data.title: RQI: What Real Estate Should Be
data.description:
We all have an idea of what a real estate investment should look like. For most folks, this looks something like owning a cash flow producing asset that delivers rent checks. The combination of monthly rent and long term capital appreciation supported by factors like market rent growth and inflation is what makes real estate such an appealing investment.

Whether people are looking to buy a house to rent, apartments, an office building, the warehouse, or something else entirely, they quickly learn that a commercial asset is much more like running a business than a passive investment. The promise of passive income is quickly spoiled by the need to retain and vacant space, perform repairs and maintenance, another task to quickly eat into your time let alone bottom line. This leads people to the world of REITs.

Although individual REITs are diversified across hundreds or thousands of assets, there is still a clear degree of business risk. Perhaps the best example is a company like Alexandria Real Estate (ARE), which has seen massive price declines and dividend cuts despite still owning high quality assets.

The past five years have uncovered a volatile REIT environment where many fan favorites have run into trouble. It seems like REIT picking has become considerably more difficult. So, where do we go from here?

I propose you consider the Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) from Cohen & Steers (CNS).

Why RQI?

In the past, I have covered RQI from a variety of angles. While touching on different parts of the fund, my core thesis has remained that it is simply the closest thing we can get to what I imagine a real estate investment should be.

Zooming back just a couple of paragraphs, we all want something that includes high current income with long-term capital appreciation. This is just what RQI has been able to deliver.

RQI is structured as a closed end fund with a flexibly assembled portfolio of real estate companies with a smaller sleeve of debt and preferreds.

The fund also dabbles in listed and unlisted options as well as other derivatives associated with the fund’s borrowing. Along the lines of borrowing, the fund is leveraged with roughly one-third of total assets being borrowed.

Since RQI is a closed end fund, the manager uses a managed distribution policy. This means sending a level monthly dividend to shareholders of the fund. In the case of RQI, this distribution remained remarkably consistent over time. In 2016, RQI opted to switch to monthly distributions, setting a $0.08 per share per month payout. For the next decade, RQI sent shareholders the same monthly distribution, every month of every year. Occasionally, there was a special year-end distribution as well.

This year, the fund increased the monthly distribution to $0.09 per share per month, an increase of 12.5%. Again, this degree of consistency is uncommon even for closed end funds with a managed distribution policy.

The why behind RQI is relatively simple. We want dependable monthly income that amounts to a considerable long-term return. The first part is easy; the second part is considerably harder.

Performance

Performance for closed end funds is a tricky gambit. Essentially all closed end funds have high distribution yields because of the tax structure associated with a RIC. But current yield is not the best assessor of long-term return. These funds, like REITs themselves, are forced to distribute essentially all income and capital gains to shareholders or face penalties.

This means that over time, many funds that opt for a level distribution policy end up overdistributing. Closed end funds that hold debt typically see a more stable net asset value, while funds that hold equities see their net asset value become considerably more volatile. Over time, this volatility can cause net asset value erosion if the manager chooses to maintain a level distribution policy. NAV erosion is a tricky and often misused term.

NAV erosion occurs when a fund overdistributes to shareholders consistently over time. This means a piece of the fund’s net asset value beyond earnings is going back to shareholders to pay them their distribution. Do this enough times and the small amount of NAV being sent to shareholders begins to compound. This overdistribution in real estate closed end funds is best

In the world of real estate funds, this overdistribution is best illustrated in funds like the CBRE Global Real Estate Income Fund (IGR) or the abrdn Global Premier Properties Fund (AWP), both of which pay double-digit distribution yields. Over the past ten years, both funds have seen their net asset values decline considerably.

While their distributions have remained relatively stable or even increased, net asset value has declined to offset total return.

RQI does something different. The fund’s distribution yield is considerably lower than either competing fund at 8.8%. Since 2013, the fund's net asset value has grown by around 20%.

Over the long term, this has led RQI to outperform competing funds, despite paying a lower distribution.

With net asset value remaining more consistent over time, it leads me to view RQI as a superior investment compared to either of the two competing funds.

Under The Hood

Something you’ll notice quickly is that the 8.4% distribution is still considerably higher than the REIT market more broadly and the fund’s top holdings. Right now, the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) pays a TTM yield of 3.73%, less than half of the distribution yield of RQI.

RQI is an actively managed fund, which means the managers are constantly buying and selling stocks, bonds, and options of companies within their investment sphere. This creates ongoing capital gains and losses, which are taken into consideration to align with the fund’s distribution. RQI collects dividends from REIT holdings, interest from bonds, and premiums from options, which are included in the distribution. Supplementing to close the gap are these capital gains reaped from buying and selling REIT stock within the portfolio.

Following a record year for some companies in the portfolio, it appears as though RQI is reaping some serious gains.

As it stands, the first six distributions of 2026 have all been funded by long term capital gains, according to CEF Connect. For taxable investors, this is a considerable benefit built into the distribution that boosts taxable equivalent yield even further.

Some might be asking where the gains are coming from. There are actually some pockets of real estate that have been quite strong. For example, Welltower (WELL) remains the largest holding at 16.3% of assets.

WELL has seen a generational boost over the past three years. Senior housing has seen extraordinary demand shifts in the past few years, and owner-operators of all shapes and sizes are reaping some extraordinary benefits. WELL is the largest senior living landlord and currently trades at a white knuckle valuation of 40X trailing FFO. Rising over 50% in the past year, WELL has some big shoes to fill in terms of anticipated growth with the current dividend yield sitting at just 1.4%.

Beyond senior living, AI continues to propel demand for data centers and infrastructure REITs as secondary beneficiaries. Looking in the top ten holdings of RQI, some of the largest names like Digital Realty Trust (DLR) and American Tower Corporation (AMT) account for another 15% of assets.

Most important is that the portfolio is constantly changing. Several years ago, industrial packed the top holdings of the portfolio as the demand wave following the pandemic boosted industrial REITs. Today, industrial holdings have trimmed down to just Prologis (PLD) in the top ten holdings at 7%. While management is moving the needle, market shifts alone are responsible for a large piece of the portfolio’s change. In the case of WELL, RQI owned the same number of shares from Q3 to Q4, but the concentration increased considerably because WELL shares appreciated by 30%.

When you look under the hood of RQI, you realize that the investment is seriously diversified. The fund has over 200 individual holdings, each of which stems from a diversified operation. This means exposure to a truly global pool of assets managed by an equally wide pool of asset managers.

Conclusion

For a hands-off real estate opportunity, it really doesn’t get much better than RQI. The fund has a stable net asset value and a distribution, which has led to an attractive total return profile over time.

Even better, RQI continues to be an outperformer, besting VNQ over most time horizons, including the past decade.

With a small distribution bump going into this year, the proof is in the pudding that RQI is cut from another cloth when it comes to real estate closed end funds. Stacked up against funds like AWP and IGR, RQI looks like a superior opportunity when considering net asset value stability. RQI continues to earn a Buy rating, deserving to be on your short list of real estate closed end funds.

data.title: ClearBridge International Value Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
By Sean Bogda, CFA, Grace Su, and Jean Yu, CFA, Ph.D.

Looking Through Fog of War to Secular Opportunities

Market Overview

International stocks entered 2026 with a constructive backdrop but were caught up in an equity pullback following the outbreak of the Iran conflict. Despite this, international markets proved more resilient than their U.S. counterparts. The core MSCI EAFE Index declined 1.2% for the quarter compared to a 4.3% decline for the S&P 500. Value stocks outperformed meaningfully, as the MSCI EAFE Value Index returned 2.0%, while the MSCI EAFE Growth Index declined 4.7% as higher rates and concerns around AI disintermediation pressured richly valued growth stocks and drove rotation into defensive, cash-generative sectors such as energy and materials.

Exhibit 1: MSCI Growth vs. Value Quarterly Performance

As of March 31, 2026. Source: FactSet.

The first quarter began with improving global growth expectations, supported by easing trade tensions, European fiscal stimulus and signs of stabilization in China. In Europe, prior rate cuts and Germany’s infrastructure spending began to support confidence, while in Japan, a decisive election outcome strengthened the case for fiscal expansion and reform. China’s recovery remained uneven, with weak consumption and ongoing property sector challenges, though early signs suggested the housing-driven deflationary cycle was bottoming, supported by stronger exports and investment in strategic industries such as AI and biotechnology. While optimism around AI adoption remained intact, there was a sharp bifurcation between perceived winners (hardware “picks and shovels”) and losers (incumbent software and service intermediaries) that generally benefitted ex-U.S. markets due to their greater orientation toward hardware and infrastructure businesses.

"Volatility will continue to create opportunities to deploy capital into high-quality businesses."

This improving narrative was disrupted in March by the escalation of the Iran conflict, which drove a sharp increase in energy prices and introduced new macroeconomic uncertainty. Higher oil and gas prices acted as a tax on growth, pressuring consumers and corporate margins while amplifying inflation. Markets reacted accordingly, with energy-importing regions — particularly in Asia — underperforming alongside more cost-sensitive sectors such as real estate and consumer discretionary. The inflationary impact complicated the policy outlook, as central banks including the Federal Reserve and Bank of England signaled a more cautious approach to rate cuts, while a stronger U.S. dollar further tightened global financial conditions and contributed to a more volatile, macro-driven environment.

Portfolio Performance

The ClearBridge International Value Strategy mildly underperformed its MSCI EAFE benchmark in the first quarter, primarily due to weakness in Japan.

Stock selection in materials weighed the most on relative performance. Wienerberger (WBRBY), an Austria-based producer of clay and concrete building materials, declined as weakness in residential construction across Europe and the U.S. continued to pressure volumes despite management’s efforts to control costs. Holcim (HCMLF), a Switzerland-based global supplier of cement and building solutions, also detracted as rising energy costs and inflation concerns clouded the outlook for construction activity; speculation around potential regulatory changes also raised questions about the durability of its competitive advantage in low-carbon production.

ICON (ICLR), an Ireland-domiciled contract research organization, was another notable detractor. The stock came under pressure amid concerns around slowing demand and the potential for AI to disrupt traditional drug development models. An announced investigation into accounting practices further weighed on investor confidence and management credibility, and we exited the position during the quarter.

Stock selection in industrials proved mixed. Airbus, the France-based global manufacturer of commercial aircraft, detracted following a reduction in delivery guidance driven by ongoing supply chain constraints, particularly among engine suppliers. While end-market demand remains robust, persistent bottlenecks have delayed production timelines and pushed out deliveries, weighing on near-term earnings visibility. Siemens AG (SIEGY), a Germany-based provider of industrial automation and software solutions, also detracted as concerns around the potential impact of AI on enterprise software models pressured sentiment despite stable underlying fundamentals.

Top contributor Marubeni (MARUY), a Japan-based trading and industrials company, helped to partially offset these headwinds. Its significant exposure to commodities and industrial end markets drove strong performance, benefiting from higher prices — particularly in copper — amid supply constraints and structural demand tied to electrification and infrastructure investment.

Several of our other holdings also benefited from the appreciation in commodities — particularly in energy. TotalEnergies, a France-based integrated oil and gas company, and Shell, a U.K.-based global energy major, were among the largest contributors as higher oil and natural gas prices supported earnings expectations and cash flow generation. Both companies continue to demonstrate strong capital discipline and shareholder return profiles, which were increasingly valued in a more uncertain macro environment.

Within IT, South Korean semiconductor manufacturers Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK Hynix were key contributors. Both companies are leading producers of DRAM and high-bandwidth memory used in data centers and AI applications. Persistent undersupply in memory markets has driven strong pricing power and profitability and, despite increased industry capital expenditures, long lead times for new fabrication capacity are expected to keep supply tight through at least 2027. However, our underweight allocation to Japan, and particularly our more selective exposure to AI beneficiaries outside Japan, weighed on performance, further complicated by IT services provider Fujitsu declining due to concerns around AI disruption.

Portfolio Positioning

Within financials, we initiated a position in Danske Bank (DNSKF), Denmark’s largest financial institution and a leading Nordic banking group. We believe the company’s return of accumulated excess capital to shareholders is underappreciated, while current earnings expectations appear overly conservative given the potential for improved loan growth and continued operational efficiency. This was partially funded by our exit of Rakuten Bank (RKUNY), a Japan-based digital banking platform, following renewed discussions around the reorganization of its parent company’s fintech operations, creating governance and dilution risks alongside an increasingly competitive deposit growth backdrop.

We improved the quality of our exposure to industrials and factory automation by swapping Japanese holding THK (THKLY) for fellow Japanese industrial SMC. Having seen a recovery in end-market demand and the divestiture of a lower-margin business, motion control manufacturer THK approached our estimate of fair value, prompting us to reallocate into SMC, a manufacturer of pneumatic equipment. SMC’s higher-quality business and strong market position offer greater upside potential, and we believe current valuations reflect trough conditions following a prolonged industry downturn, with a recovery in demand and lower capital intensity expected to drive improved margins and cash flow.

Outlook

The initial fog of war has shaken investor confidence toward international markets, particularly as higher energy prices and elevated inflation may keep central banks restrictive, tightening financial conditions, while a stronger U.S. dollar pressures global liquidity and emerging markets.

However, we believe it is important not to lose sight of the broader set of growth drivers underpinning the case for international value stocks. Structural trends, including investment in energy security, defense and infrastructure, should support demand across industrial and real asset–oriented sectors, which are much larger components of non-U.S. markets.

Additionally, the AI boom remains in early innings and is driving an investment cycle orders of magnitude beyond that of the prior few decades. As AI transitions from being a deflationary, margin-preserving tool to a productivity-enhancing growth driver, investors may also begin to reconsider which profit pools and business moats are truly defensible and seek broadening of exposure beyond U.S. mega cap technology. While uncertainty persists, we remain focused on these important secular themes and look for opportunities to deploy capital into leading beneficiaries as they become attractively valued.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge International Value Strategy underperformed its MSCI EAFE benchmark during the first quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy had gains across four of the 11 sectors in which it was invested, with the energy sector being the greatest contributor and the consumer discretionary sector the largest detractor.

On a relative basis, overall stock selection effects weighed on performance, but were partially offset by positive sector allocation effects. Stock selection in the materials, health care and utilities sectors weighed on performance. Conversely, stock selection in the IT and real estate sectors, as well as overweights to the energy and materials sectors, proved beneficial.

On a regional basis, stock selection in Japan, Europe Ex U.K. and the U.K. and an underweight to Asia Ex Japan detracted from performance. An overweight to emerging markets, underweight to Europe Ex U.K. and stock selection in North America contributed positively.

On an individual stock basis, Marubeni, Samsung Electronics, TotalEnergies (TTE), SK Hynix and not owning SAP (SAP) were the greatest contributors to relative performance. The largest relative detractors were Fujitsu (FJTSF), ICON, Airbus (EADSF), Siemens and not owning ASML (ASML).

During the quarter, in addition to the transactions mentioned above, we initiated positions in Smurfit Westrock (SW) in the materials sector, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) in the utilities sector and TFI International (TFII) in the industrials sector. We exited positions in Sanofi (SNY) in the health care sector and Cellnex Telecom (CLNXF) in the communication services sector.

Sean Bogda, CFA, Portfolio Manager

Grace Su, Portfolio Manager

Jean Yu, CFA, Ph.D., Portfolio Manager

data.title: Rackspace Technology: Right Strategy, Uncertain Execution
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Rackspace Technology, Inc. (RXT) operates in a growing cloud services market, but its position in the value chain is structurally weak. This was the result of limited pricing power, weak margins, and an inability to deliver sustained revenue growth since its 2020 IPO.

The company is attempting a turnaround by exiting low-margin contracts, improving service mix, and moving towards higher-value services. However, this transition is still at an early stage. As such, Rackspace represents a high-execution-risk turnaround, where upside depends on strong execution.

Business background

The company first IPO in 2008 but was taken private in 2016. The present form was re-listed in 2020. Over the past decade, Rackspace transformed from a data center hosting company into a multi-cloud services provider.

Rackspace is today a multi-cloud services provider. It does not own the cloud infrastructure like AWS, but rather it provides a service layer that helps companies use the cloud infrastructure effectively.

I would like to think of Rackspace as an outsourced IT/cloud expert that designs, runs, and optimizes its customers’ cloud environment. Its revenue comes from

  1. recurring fees for managing and supporting customers’ cloud environments
  2. providing non-recurring professional services such as cloud migration, application modernization, and increasingly AI/data platform engineering.

However, it reports its results based on 2 segments - private cloud and public cloud.

The former is Rackspace’s legacy infrastructure business, while public cloud is its business that is built on top of hyperscale cloud providers such as AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.

As illustrated in Chart 1, the public cloud business has grown from 49 % of its total revenue in 2021 to 63 % in 2025. The company remains a US-centric business, with the US averaging about 69% of the past 5 years' total revenue.

Market challenges

The cloud computing market is a growing one, as illustrated by the following:

Cloud High-Performance Computing Market size was valued at USD 48.15 Billion in 2024 and is projected to reach USD 82.73 Billion by 2032, growing at a CAGR of 7% from 2026 to 2032.

Verified Market Research

While the cloud market is a growing one, the niche that Rackspace serves - multi-cloud services and managed cloud - is structurally challenging. Don’t get me wrong. This niche market is still a double-digit growing one as illustrated by the following:

Multi cloud management market was over USD 13.48 billion in 2025…witnessing over 27.6 % CAGR during the forecast period (2025 to 2035).

Research Nester

And:

The global cloud managed services market size was valued at USD 155.95 billion in 2025… exhibiting a CAGR of 16.00% during the forecast period (2026 to 2034).

Fortune Business

However, the same research highlights significant execution challenges. This is because the managed services market is highly competitive and dominated by large IT service providers, with limited differentiation and pricing power.

To compete successfully in this niche, the company needs strong moats. This is challenging for Rackspace as its competitive advantages seem to come from its multi-cloud expertise across major hyperscale platforms, as well as long-standing enterprise relationships and a reputation for high-touch customer support.

These point to customer embeddedness. The company does not seem to have other moats, such as proprietary platforms or infrastructure ownership (to provide a cost advantage).

As such, its competitive edge does not look durable. Its capabilities can be replicated.

These suggest that Rackspace’s competitive advantages are primarily operational rather than structural. In my quantitative analysis later, I will show that it does not have the pricing power that comes with a strong moat.

Operating performance

Since its IPO, the company has not delivered any revenue growth. While revenue grew from 2020 to 2022, it declined thereafter, such that the 2025 revenue was marginally lower than that in 2020.

The company attributed this to:

  • Structural decline in the legacy private cloud business and/or intentional exit from low-quality business.
  • Market conditions and competitive pressure.

Not only was there hardly any revenue growth, but the gross profit margin and the corresponding contribution margin have also declined, as illustrated in Chart 2.

Notes to Chart 2:

a) Fixed cost covers Selling, General and & Administration, Provision of bad debts, R&D expenses, and Depreciation & Amortization

b) Variable cost is defined as Cost of Sales excluding Depreciation & Amortization

c) Contribution is defined as Revenue – Variable Cost with Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue

While there was a corresponding decline in the fixed cost margin (as per Chart 2, left), the reality is that the gross profit margin was never above the fixed cost margin over the past 8 years.

To be profitable, the company needs to operate significantly above its breakeven level. I estimated that, based on the 2025 margin and cost, the company needs $2.9 billion in revenue. In its February 2026 investor presentation, the company guided its 2026 revenue to be between $2.6 billion and $2.7 billion.

To be profitable at these lower revenues, the company needs to either improve its gross profit margin (and the corresponding contribution margin) or lower its fixed cost margin.

The challenge for the former is its weak moat. The company does not appear to have strong pricing power. Its own filings acknowledge that larger competitors may use aggressive pricing, potentially forcing Rackspace to lower prices to remain competitive.

As such, any improvement in profitability at lower revenues is more likely to come from a better revenue mix and lower fixed costs than from materially higher pricing.

The fixed cost challenge is that the 33% fixed cost margin in 2025 has to come down to below the 2025 gross profit margin of 19%. This is a big gap, as historically the fixed cost margin has declined by about 4% annually. You can understand why this fixed cost reduction route can be challenging for the next few years.

On the positive side, there is some operating leverage in the business. This is because fixed costs accounted for about one-third of total costs in 2025 (Chart 2, right). This implies that revenue growth could have a positive impact on profitability.

The big picture is that while revenue growth could help close the profitability gap, it is unlikely to be sufficient on its own. Sustainable profitability would require a combination of revenue growth, improved service mix (to maintain or improve the margin), and continued cost discipline.

For example, assuming a 3% decline in fixed cost margin and maintaining the 2025 gross profit margin, the breakeven revenue would be about $2.8 billion, implying the need for approximately 4% revenue growth.

Financial position

While Rackspace was not profitable post its 2020 IPO, I would rate the company as financially sound based on the following. This financial strength would provide the company with some breathing space as it addresses the various profitability issues:

1. As of the end of Dec 2025, it had $ 110 million in cash and short-term investments, equivalent to 4 % of its total assets.

2. Post-IPO, it generated positive cash flow from operations annually. Over this period, it generated $ 1.3 billion cash flow from operations compared to the total loss after tax of $ 3.2 billion. This is a good cash conversion rate.

3. While it was not profitable since its 2020 IPO, it had a good capital allocation plan. Its cash flow from operations was enough to support its capital expenditure and acquisitions. Refer to Table 1.

4. From 2020 to 2025, it had negative annual reinvestment. I defined Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisition – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.

The negative was because the Depreciation & Amortization more than offset the amount spent on CAPEX & Acquisition and changes in Net Working Capital. This meant that the company did not have to incur funds to maintain the business. Of course, over the long run, I would I would expect this to go up to one driven by the fundamental growth equation of growth = reinvestment rate X return.

Its main negative point was its high debt capital ratio of 156% at the end of Dec 2025. But this was due to the negative capital (due to cumulative losses).

Peer comparison

I compared Rackspace’s performance with the following peers with a similar product-market mix.

  • Accenture: Rackspace lists this company among the large global IT systems integrators it competes with.
  • Capgemini (CAPMF) is named by Rackspace as a competitor in the traditional global IT systems integration business.
  • Cognizant: Rackspace competes with this company in consulting and outsourcing, with overlap in enterprise cloud modernization and managed services.
  • DXC Technology: Rackspace lists DXC as a global systems integrator it competes with for large-enterprise managed infrastructure and cloud transformation work.
  • Kyndryl is especially close to Rackspace in managed infrastructure, hybrid cloud, and hyperscaler-alliance work.

As shown in Table 2, Rackspace is a very small player in terms of the 2025 revenue and ranked No. 4 for the revenue growth rate from 2020 to 2025.

Notes to Table 2:

a) Except for Accenture (FYE Aug) and DXC (FYE Mar), all the others had Dec as the financial year-end

b) The values in the peer analysis were based on the financial years.

c) Capgemini data were based on Tikr.com USD conversion.

I compared Rackspace’s peer performance based on several metrics shown in Charts 3 to 5.

Compared to its peers, Rackspace’s overall performance has been weak and inconsistent.

  1. Rackspace's balance sheet position is particularly concerning, as it has the highest debt capital ratio among peers. This ratio had also deteriorated over time.
  2. Operationally, Rackspace returns on capital and EBIT margins were near the bottom of the peer group throughout the review period.
  3. Although the company recorded strong unlevered free cash flow margins in some years, this was volatile. This suggests that it did not have a stable cash-generating advantage.
  4. While losses have narrowed over time, Rackspace remains unprofitable with the weakest improvement in earnings per share among its peers.

Notes to Chart 3:

a) While Rackspace did not generate any significant revenue growth comparing 2025 to 2202, 2 other companies experienced revenue decline.

b) Rackspace has the highest (worst) debt capital ratio over the review period. In fact, the ratio increased (got worse) from 2020 to 2025 compared to its peers. This was probably due to its accumulated losses.

Notes to Chart 4:

a) Except for 2 years when it ranked No. 4 (2021 and 2023), Rackspace generally occupied the bottom 2 positions where in came to the return on capital.

b) Except for 2021, when it ranked No. 4, Rackspace occupied the bottom 2 positions for the EBIT margin over the review period.

Notes to Chart 5:

a) On an overall average basis, Rackspace ranked No. 1 for the unlevered free cash flow margin. But it also had one of the more volatile performances for this metric, ranking No. 1 for 3 years and ranking No. 6 for 1 year.

b) While Rackspace was unprofitable post-2020, it managed to reduce its annual loss per share from 2020 to 2025. But compared to peers, it ranked the worst in terms of the improvement in the annual EPS over the review period.

Prospects

As illustrated by the peer analysis, this is a viable business for those with the right positioning and economics.

Peers like Accenture succeed because they sell high-value transformation work and not commoditized support. They have scale and brand credibility, enabling them to focus on large enterprise deals.

In contrast, Rackspace seems to have larger exposure to lower-value managed services, lacks a scale advantage, and is dragged down by the legacy business.

Rackspace’s turnaround depends on shifting from a commoditized cloud operator to a higher-value platform engineering and advisory provider. At the same time, it should work on reducing its cost base and exiting low-margin work.

The positive sign is that there are clear indications that Rackspace is trying to move in this direction:

  • “Public Cloud Mix Shift — Strategic exit of low-margin government contract and pivot toward higher-margin services.” (February 2026 Investor’s Presentation)
  • "AI as Structural Growth Driver — Building a scalable platform engineering model and advancing strategic partnerships.” (February 2026 Investor’s Presentation)
  • “This requires us to anticipate and respond to customer demands…by improving our cost structure.” (2025 Form 10K)

While Rackspace has identified the right path, there is no track record yet of translating this strategy into sustained revenue growth and improved profitability. The turnaround is still a work in progress.

Valuation

The value of Rackspace would depend on its path to profitability. Given the lack of a track record, rather than try to estimate the intrinsic value, I decided to reverse-engineer the margin price of $1.24 per share (15 April 2026).

On such a basis, I found that the market price reflects the following picture.

  1. The base revenue would be the 2025 revenue. This will grow at 1% in Year 1 and thereafter be at the long-term GDP growth rate of 4%.
  2. The base contribution margin would be the 2024 and 2025 average value. This would improve proportionately to be 10% higher in Year 6.
  3. The base fixed cost would be the 2025 value and grow at 4% CAGR.
  4. In the profitable years, the tax would be the nominal tax rate of 21%
  5. The reinvestment would be zero.

Valuation model

I reverse-engineered Rackspace based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) valuation model as shown in Table 3.

Notes to Table 3:

a. Projected

b. Pegged to growth rate

c. Assumed proportionate change

d. Assumed long-term growth rate

e. Revenue X Margin and after accounting for Other costs

f. Assumed zero reinvestment

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at the terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt

The WACC was based on the Google Search for “Rackspace WACC” as summarized in Table 4.

Risks and limitations

Given the lack of a track record, I have used a reverse-engineering approach to understand the assumptions implied by the current market price.

This, of course, avoids the false precision of a DCF model. But its limitations are that the derived assumptions are not forecasts but a set of conditions to match the current price. This approach does not directly estimate intrinsic value.

The results are also sensitive to assumptions around margin improvement, cost increase, and reinvestment. For example, the assumption of zero reinvestment may not hold if future growth requires continued investment in capabilities or acquisitions.

Consequently, the reverse-engineered value should be seen as a way to assess the reasonableness of market expectations.

Conclusion

Rackspace operates in a growing segment of the cloud computing market. Unfortunately, its position within the value chain is structurally weaker than that of leading peers. While companies such as Accenture have demonstrated that the business can be attractive, success in this market depends on scale and a focus on higher-value work.

Rackspace, in contrast, has struggled with marginal revenue growth and weak margins. This was due to its exposure to lower-value managed services and a declining legacy private cloud business. The result was that the company has not been profitable since its 2020 IPO.

But the company has identified the right strategic direction, and there are early signs that it is moving along this path. However, the turnaround is still at an early stage, with no clear evidence yet that it can operate beyond its breakeven level.

My reverse-engineered valuation indicates that the current market price already assumes a degree of margin improvement and cost discipline. There is no track record of this yet, and the turnaround story remains uncertain.

data.title: Protalix: Elfabrio Struggles Against Oral Competition
data.description:
Protalix BioTherapeutics, Inc. (PLX) wants to become a notable player in the Gaucher disease and Fabry disease therapeutics markets.

But on March 18, it released disappointing figures for Q4.

Let me remind you about it.

Total revenue of Protalix fell 49.9% from $17.85 million in Q3 last year to $9.12 million, missing the consensus estimate by $5.24 million.

And its non-GAAP EPS also came in below the consensus estimate, as in the past 4 quarters, but this time by 8 cents.

Thus, is the competition in this therapeutic area and other risks, which I'll discuss later, justifying a Hold or Buy rating, according to my ALLKA 4D rating?

The partnership with Chiesi leaves much to be desired

On October 18, 2017, Protalix signed a deal worth up to $370 million with Chiesi to develop PRX-102, now known as Elfabrio [pegunigalsidase alfa], and commercialize it outside the U.S.

Then, on July 24 of the next year, Protalix expanded the agreement, granting the rights of Elfabrio in the U.S., under which it is eligible to receive up to $805 million.

And speaking in simple words, pegunigalsidase alfa is an ERT [enzyme replacement therapy] approved two years ago, on May 10, by the FDA for the treatment of Fabry disease. For this milestone, Protalix received $20 million.

And by the way, 5 days earlier, on May 15, the European Commission also gave the green light to Elfabrio for this indication.

But before I analyze the performance of this ERT, I want to briefly explain what Fabry disease is.

It is a rare inherited disorder caused by deficient activity of an enzyme called alpha-GAL [alpha-galactosidase A].

When this enzyme is deficient, the body is unable to break down the fatty substance, globotriaosylceramide.

Then, this substance accumulates in cells, leading to life-threatening problems, including an enlarged heart, irregular heartbeat, myocardial infarction, and kidney failure.

Well, according to Orphanet, the global prevalence of Fabry disease at birth is about 1 case per 15,000, which, in my view, is one of the factors that attracts Big Pharma to develop medications against it.

But that's not all.

Also, most approved drugs have an "orphan drug designation," which gives them a seven-year exclusivity period after approval.

To my regret, Elfabrio has a problem here.

I could not find any mention of it in the FDA's databases.

And more importantly, the multidisciplinary review explicitly states that PLX/Chiesi's drug has no orphan designation.

On the other hand, using information from Protalix's 10-K and Google Patents, I found that of the current patents covering Elfabrio, only US9194011B2 [or PCT/IL2011/000209] is active.

It expires on November 17, 2030.

Yes, Protalix filed application number PCT/Il2018/050018 in 2018, but it has not yet been approved as you can see below (highlighted in orange).

Thus, I believe pegunigalsidase alfa's patent protection is far from perfect. However, this could change if PCT/Il2018/050018 is approved, which will protect Elfabrio from “cheap copies” until 2038.

And given everything I've said so far, did Chiesi make a mistake by making a deal with Protalix BioTherapeutics?

The only thing that can help answer this question is pegunigalsidase alfa sales.

So, it generated about $3.96 million for Protalix in Q4, down 74.1% year-on-year and 54.8% QoQ. Moreover, as you can see in the chart below, Elfabrio sales began to decline sharply in the second half of 2024.

I believe the key reason for this negative trend is competition with BioMarin/Amicus's Galafold [migalastat] (BMRN) (FOLD), which has a strong competitive advantage: it's administered orally, rather than being injected every 2 weeks, as in the case with Elfabrio.

This is reflected in Galafold sales, which totaled $150.2 million in Q4, up 17.8% year-on-year.

Sales of Elelyso are under pressure in the Gaucher disease treatment market

I believe the revenue miss, quite substantial, is also due to weaker demand for Elelyso [taliglucerase alfa] outside of Brazil.

This enzyme replacement therapy [ERT] is commercialized by Pfizer (PFE) under a deal concluded more than 15 years ago, on November 30, 2009.

Protalix's revenue from Elelyso sales was about $2.8 million, down 0.3% QoQ. In my view, this drop reflects increased competition in the Gaucher disease drugs market, mainly from Sanofi's Cerezyme/Cerdelga (SNY).

In my opinion, its performance can be called catastrophic, especially considering that Elelyso was first approved by the FDA on May 1, 2012.

In general, I'd also like to point out before moving on to the "Risks" section that Elelyso is also being commercialized in Brazil in partnership with Fiocruz. But things are much worse there.

Taliglucerase alfa's net revenue was only $1.94 million.

Risks

Now, it's time for my table with key risks.

One of them is the decline in Protalix's total cash and short-term investments in the fourth quarter by $4.5 million year-on-year to $30.3 million.

Well, it received $25 million from Chiesi following the EC's approval of a every-4-weeks dosing regimen for Elfabrio on March 9.

But given PLX's historical rate of cash burn, as well as increased R&D costs due to the development of PRX-115 and PRX-119, it may require a cash infusion, for example, through a secondary public offering in Q4 2027.

Takeaway

But, in addition to Elelyso and Elfabrio, Protalix has two product candidates, PRX-115 and PRX-119.

Here, I want to focus your attention on PRX-115, since PRX-119 is still in preclinical studies.

PRX-115 is a chemically modified enzyme, uricase, being developed to treat a painful form of arthritis called uncontrolled gout.

And one of the causes of gout is when levels of uric acid in the blood rise above 6 mg/dL.

All this ultimately leads to the deposition of monosodium urate crystals between joints.

Yes, I agree that in a Phase 1 study [NCT05745727], PRX-115 demonstrated that it reduced urate concentrations below 6.0 mg/dL.

But again, the problem is in the details. Only two of its most concentrated doses [Cohort 6 and Cohort 7] showed strong results.

What I mean is that a single dose of PRX-115 at these doses maintained levels at 6 mg/dL for up to six weeks after administration.

And this is important.

Because firstly, it must have at least some competitive advantage over Amgen's Krystexxa (AMGN), which is administered once every 2 weeks. By the way, its sales were $435 million in Q4, up 35.9% quarter-over-quarter.

However, given that Protalix's cash and short-term investments at the end of December were only $30.3 million, with a negative EBIT, this is a problem for investors.

Results of the Phase 2 trial [NCT07280156] for PRX-115 won't be available until at least 2028.

And without strong clinical data and the weak sales of Elelyso/Elfabrio, I believe few institutional investors will want to invest in Protalix now.

As a result, I believe Protalix stock's risk/reward profile is unfavorable, especially given its stock price has risen 41.7% over the past 6 months.

data.title: Richemont: 3 Factors To Consider Before Buying
data.description:
2026 hasn't gone well for Compagnie Financière Richemont SA's (OTCPK:CFRUY) (OTCPK:CFRHF) so far. It's seen a bigger decline than both the S&P Global Luxury Index (SPGLGUN) and the S&P 500 (SP500) (see Chart 1 below). Disappointing as this is, two trends provide some comfort:

  • CFRUY's drop is better placed than the other three big luxury fashion stocks, Hermès (OTCPK:HESAY), LVMH (OTCPK:LVMUY), and Kering (OTCPK:PPRUY), in that it has seen a smaller drop (see Chart 2 below).
  • The weakness in price performance for the luxury set started in earnest only from February end, when the US-Iran war began, indicating that it's more event- than fundamentals-driven.

These raise the question of whether CFRUY can see a turnaround now. This question acquires validity in light of the notable 12% gains for the stock in the past month, even as it still trades below levels seen at the start of 2026. The answer is both yes and no, as there are arguments on both sides. These arguments are based on three key factors that can determine what's next for the stock are discussed below.

#1. Luxury Industry in the Macro Context

As peace still remains elusive, increased macroeconomic uncertainty that has already shown up in an inflation uptick calls for a relook at the best stocks to buy. Luxury stocks' relatively slowdown-proof nature certainly makes them attractive, but there's another side to the story too. As discussed below.

The Upside: Unlike other consumer discretionary stocks, luxury stocks are ones to watch, if not outright buy, in times of macroeconomic uncertainty. Like right now. This uncertainty's first sign was the oil price shock, which then resulted in a jump in inflation numbers in March. From a big picture perspective, even though inflation is still in check, until peace returns for good, there's a risk to it.

Luxury companies like Richemont are well placed to manage this risk, with their healthy operating profit margin at 20% for FY25 (year ending March 2025). Not only does it have the leeway to absorb higher costs, but it can, importantly, pass on costs more easily to consumers as well.

The Downside: The argument might hold true during regular times, but what of when there's a luxury market slowdown underway already? Since the post-COVID-19 spending spree ended, the luxury sector has been firmly in slowdown mode over the past couple of years, with signs of recovery visible only in recent months.

One of the arguments for the luxury market slowdown is the increase in prices when demand was high. According to a McKinsey and Company report, 80% of the growth in the industry over the 2019 to 2023 period was on account of price increases, while volume growth contributed far less. However, since then, price increases started backfiring. As the same report notes, higher prices led to reduced demand. The key point here is that even with strong margins, luxury companies might not have pricing power this time around if inflation rises.

#2. Sales Growth Recovery

Richemont's highlight feature is its resilient sales, which have continued to grow despite market weakness. But geopolitical stresses combined with macroeconomic disturbances do put revenues at risk, as discussed below.

The Upside: At a time when the luxury market is in a slump, as evident from LVMH's nearly unchanged organic revenues in 2025 and Kering's even worse performance last year, Richemont's continued growth is encouraging. Sure, it has slowed down. In FY23, for example, sales grew by a robust 19%, a number that dwindled to just 4% by FY25. However, even the fact that it continues to remain well in the positive territory is a positive indeed.

Moreover, signs of a robust recovery underway are obvious. In Q3 FY26 (October-December 2025), Richemont's sales growth at constant prices was at 11% YoY. This was a slight improvement from the 10% YoY increase seen in H1 FY26. Specifically, continued growth in its big Asia Pacific market was encouraging since it reflects well on the important Chinese market.

The Downside: There are risks to growth, though, most significantly from the Middle East & Africa market, which brings in some 10% of the company's revenues. In Q3 FY26, it saw the highest growth among all regions of 20% YoY, continuing the trend from H1 FY26. However, with the US-Iran war underway, which has affected the Middle East significantly, weakness in the region is possible for Q4 FY26. Also, less concerning but still worth pointing out is some softening in European growth to 8% YoY (H1 FY26: 11% YoY).

#3. Two Sides to the Market Multiples

To estimate the forward P/E for FY26, here I've left the assumptions unchanged from last time. The projection for profit for continuing operations, which is also assumed to double as the profit for the period this past financial year, is at EUR 3.9 billion (USD 4.6 billion), and the forward P/E ratio is now at 25.1x.

For FY27, revenue growth is assumed to remain at 5% at actual exchange rates in FY27, the same as that for FY26. However, the profit margin is reduced from 18% earlier to 17.2% now. This is because the earlier underlying assumption that costs will continue to soften on falling inflation no longer stands. The profit figure for FY27 comes to EUR 4.1 billion (USD 4.8 billion), and the forward P/E to 23.9x.

The upside: CFRUY's forward P/E doesn't look bad at all when compared to peers. The average forward ratio for the big four luxury fashion stocks (including CFRUY) is at 31.7x for the present year, indicating that CFRUY is undervalued. Further, it remains below the luxury average of 25.8x for the next year as well, though to a smaller extent.

The Downside: Compared with its own past average, however, the stock does less well. CFRUY's five-year average sits at 21x, which indicates that for both FY26 and FY27, the stock is overpriced.

What's Next?

With its neat gains in the past month coupled with continued weakness YTD, it can be tempting to buy CFRUY now. But as the discussion above reveals, there are arguments both for and against buying the stock.

The company's sales growth recovery, its market multiples compared with peers, and even the potential for luxury market resilience in a slowdown work well for it. But the ongoing luxury market slump, potential weakness in its fast-growing Middle East and Africa market, as well as high P/E compared with its own past averages, temper the case for it.

I'm retaining a Hold rating on the stock until its FY26 results release in May, which can provide a fuller picture of at least what to expect in the current financial year. Also, by then hopefully the geopolitical stresses would have resolved to provide greater market stability. But we have to wait and watch.

data.title: ClearBridge International Growth ADR Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
By Elisa Mazen | Michael Feldman, CFA | Michael Testorf, CFA | Pawel Wroblewski, CFA

Resetting as Growth Becomes More Attractive

Market and Performance Overview

International equities declined meaningfully in March as the U.S. and Israel’s military conflict with Iran escalated materially through the month, leading to mixed results for the first quarter after two positive months. The core benchmark MSCI EAFE Index finished down 1.2% yet extended its leadership over the S&P 500 Index (-4.3%). Value stocks outside the U.S. continued to exhibit strength, with the MSCI EAFE Value Index managing a gain of 2.0% for the quarter, outperforming the MSCI EAFE Growth Index (-4.7%) by 670 basis points. Value is ahead of growth by over 1,700 bps for the trailing 12 months.

While international markets had been preparing for a period of lower rates, which would have been beneficial to growth stocks generally, rates began to move higher following the start of the Middle East conflict. Equity markets are still grappling with long-term implications of these moves and, most importantly, their duration.

Exhibit 1: MSCI Growth vs. Value Performance

With continued headwinds from value leadership, especially in energy, the ClearBridge International Growth ADR Strategy underperformed its primary MSCI EAFE benchmark but outperformed its secondary MSCI EAFE Growth benchmark.

The biggest obstacle affecting relative performance was a lack of traditional energy exposure in a three-month period which saw the energy sector soar 40%. Given the selloff in March, higher-beta names in areas such as financials, consumer discretionary and information technology (IT) names were most impacted.

"We are seeking out companies supporting energy security and benefiting from increased fiscal spending in Europe."

The Iran conflict created immediate oil and gas supply disruptions that directly affect an energy-dependent Europe already struggling with the cutoff of Russian energy due to the war in Ukraine. Closure of the Strait of Hormuz also raised the risk of higher inflation from commodity price spikes and caused the European Central Bank to reverse course on what was expected to be a continued program of rate cuts. These rapid and dramatic changes, combined with profit taking after strong performance in 2025, sent the financials sector down 3.7% for the quarter and pressured portfolio holdings Adyen (ADYEY), Intesa Sanpaolo (ISNPY) and NatWest Group (NWG).

Banks, particularly European banks — which represent our largest exposure — saw some profit taking after a standout 2025 and have also served as a source of funding for banks in other regions, insurance companies and stock exchanges, all of which are perceived as more defensive. Notably, Australian and Canadian banks have shown resilience, being less affected by rising oil and gas prices or potential rationing. Over the quarter, we trimmed some of our banking exposure as several holdings approached their target prices.

The launch of Claude Cowork and other generative AI tools in the quarter heightened concerns that AI-native solutions could alter competitive dynamics in IT, increase pricing pressure, extend the sales cycle or compress long-term growth rates. As a result, the software sector — historically viewed as stable and durable due to high recurring revenue and strong retention — experienced broad-based multiple contraction. This dynamic impacted nearly all software companies, including those we believe are relatively well-positioned to benefit from AI or are less susceptible to disruption. We broadly reduced the portfolio’s software exposure in the quarter, including cutting Germany’s SAP (SAP) by roughly two-thirds.

Despite this short-term weakness, we are optimistic about the outlook for international growth stocks and confident that the work we have put into the portfolio in recent quarters positions the Strategy very well for improved outcomes going forward. After an extended period of value outperformance, we believe growth stocks have given up much of their valuation premium.

Exhibit 2: International Growth Premiums Have Compressed

Portfolio Positioning

We saw indiscriminate selling in March across our investable universe, especially among what we consider emerging growth companies with higher revenue growth and innovative business models that carry higher risk. We used the spike in volatility to repurchase shares of video streamer Spotify (SPOT). Prior to the conflict-driven selloff, we exited Shopify (SHOP) and MercadoLibre (MELI) as they reached our price targets.

We are also seeking out companies supporting energy security and benefiting from increased fiscal spending in Europe; these include defense stocks and those enabling electric grid infrastructure such as new buy French utility Engie (ENGIY). Supporting our expectation of higher infrastructure spending in Europe as well was the purchase of U.K.-based global mining company Rio Tinto (RIO), the world’s largest miner by revenue and second largest by profit. While its business has historically been dominated by iron ore, more than half of its future profits will be generated from copper, aluminum, lithium and other industrial metals in high demand.

In addition, we continued to move more meaningfully into Japan with five new positions that reflect our confidence in a number of tailwinds: the country is an outlier in developed markets in that interest rates are rising, which should meaningfully boost net interest margins for banks; ongoing governance reforms are driving higher returns on equity and increasing shareholder-friendly actions such as stock buybacks; and expansionary fiscal policy under the consolidating Takaichi administration that should support domestic demand, defense spending and technology investment.

We purchased ORIX (IX), which provides capital and financing to public and private sector clients and operates its own real estate and renewable energy assets; Pan Pacific International Holdings (DQJCY), an operator of discount retail chains; Shin-Etsu Chemical (SHECY), a supplier of specialty materials to the semiconductor, construction and pharmaceutical sectors; and Sumitomo Electric Industries (SMTOY), a manufacturer of electrical components that power grid connection, transmission and data networking equipment. A portion of these buys came were funded from the sale of European aerospace and defense contractor Airbus (EADSF) which was approaching our price target.

Within IT, we are roughly neutral to the semiconductor capital equipment industry after taking some profits in the strong-performing ASML (ASML). We diversified exposure to this critical picks and shovels area of AI buildouts with the first-quarter additions of ASM International (ASMIY) and Lasertec (LSRCY). ASM is the leader in atomic layer deposition, a precise deposition technique required in the most advanced semiconductors. The Dutch company’s main clients are logic foundries Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Samsung (SSNLF) and Intel (INTC), as well as memory foundries SK Hynix and Micron Technology (MU). Foundries and wafer fab equipment providers like the ASM work closely, which gives the firm insight into the customer's innovation and product road maps. Japan’s Lasertec is a pure-play leader in inspection and measurement equipment that is well-positioned to benefit from the emerging growth drivers of extreme ultraviolet (EUV) memory adoption and AI infrastructure. We took advantage of an attractive entry point given that the company is at trough earnings with order recovery ahead.

The sales of Japanese IT consultant Nomura Research Institute (NRILY) and U.K. business information provider RELX (RELX) were driven in part by negative sentiment over AI disintermediation risks that are currently overwhelming the fundamentals of these businesses. We exited Israeli security software maker Check Point (CHKP) because our thesis on revenue growth acceleration is taking longer to play out than expected.

Outlook

War has changed cost economics around the world, which we know is not good for Europe due to its energy dependence, as we saw during the early stages of the Russia-Ukraine war. How long this lasts and what the result will be has yet to be determined, especially with Iran controlling the Strait of Hormuz, and we must look at potential impacts across several areas. We are analyzing how inflationary pressures from higher commodity prices could impact consumers, as well as what they mean for input prices for industrial and materials companies.

We have also been carefully vetting the portfolio to be better in tune with the current risk environment and ensure we are appropriately positioned for the next leg of growth. We have reduced our IT overweight while continuing to add to utilities and materials. We see these structural companies as the new growth stocks and have seen early signs validating our thesis. We have also been moving more meaningfully into Japan, and to a lesser extent Australia, highlighting an overall priority to add in places where we see inexpensive valuations and rising earnings.

While the Strategy has very limited exposure to oil and gas stocks — which acted as a major performance headwind in the quarter — we are also looking at our energy exposure, achieved through owning utilities and select industrials, as a way to play defense and be well-positioned to participate in a period of surging power demand. This contributed to our decision to add a position in Australian LNG company Woodside Energy during the quarter.

Portfolio Highlights

During the first quarter, the ClearBridge International Growth ADR Strategy underperformed its MSCI EAFE Index benchmark but outperformed its MSCI EAFE Growth Index secondary benchmark. On an absolute basis, the Strategy produced positive contributions across three of the 11 sectors in which it was invested: industrials, materials and utilities. The consumer discretionary and financials sectors were the chief detractors.

We have been monitoring on a regular basis and reporting to our clients on the performance of the MSCI EAFE (core), MSCI EAFE Value and MSCI EAFE Growth indexes in order to provide context on market movements for growth investors.

Relative to the MSCI EAFE Index, overall stock selection and sector allocation detracted from performance. In particular, stock selection in consumer discretionary, financials, communication services and IT, an underweight to energy and an overweight to consumer discretionary weighed on results. On the positive side, stock selection in the industrials and health care sectors proved beneficial.

Relative to the MSCI EAFE Growth Index, overall sector allocation contributed to performance. In particular, overweights to materials and utilities, an underweight to IT and stock selection in industrials and health care supported results. Conversely, stock selection in consumer discretionary, financials, consumer staples and IT and an underweight to industrials hurt performance.

On an individual stock basis, the largest contributors to returns relative to the MSCI EAFE Index in the quarter were ASML in IT, Siemens Energy and BAE Systems in industrials, Agnico Eagle Mines in materials, and AstraZeneca in health care. The greatest relative detractors were Sea Limited in consumer discretionary, Nomura Research and SAP in IT, Tencent in communication services as well as Adyen in financials.

In addition to the transactions mentioned above, we repurchased shares in Deutsche Boerse in financials. The Strategy also closed positions in Argenx in health care, Celestica in IT and Ferrari in consumer discretionary.

Elisa Mazen, Managing Director, Head of Global Growth, Portfolio Manager

Michael Feldman, CFA, Director, Portfolio Manager

Michael Testorf, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Pawel Wroblewski, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

data.title: Niagen Bioscience: How Its Shift To Telehealth Makes It A Strong Buy
data.description:
The market treats Niagen Bioscience (NAGE) as a traditional nutritional supplement company. However, it is undergoing a strategic transformation to become a leader in telehealth and longevity as a service. This sector offers exceptional profit margins and higher valuations compared to the traditional supplement industry.

The main catalyst that will drive the stock in the long term is management's plan to launch Niagen Plus injectables through its telemedicine platform in the first half of 2026 by taking advantage of the change in consumers' behavior brought about by GLP-1 weight loss drugs.

I believe the same consumers who overcame the barrier of self-injection to maintain their weight would be ready to do the same to maintain their youth. This shift should change the company's unit economics and increase the customer's long-term value, justifying re-rating the stock at HealthTech multiples. I rate the stock a Strong Buy at a price of around $6-$8 per share, representing a potential upside of 40% to 75%.

Competitive Advantage

Niagen is building a comprehensive system for Niagen Plus products to be available in 1,200 health clinics in the United States. However, what will change the game is the launch of subcutaneous injections with smaller doses of 100 milligrams at a cheaper price—less than half of in-clinic IV sessions.

This shift will reshape the company's revenue stream, as in typical e-commerce models, the cost of acquiring a customer is high compared to the purchase price, but in telehealth, the customer is obligated to pay a subscription to get the injections, which generates recurring revenue that significantly reduces the customer acquisition cost (CAC). Additionally, the direct sales to consumers through its platform should eliminate the intermediaries and distributors, ensuring that the majority of revenues are recorded as gross profit.

In addition, the company has a set of patents and clinical research that protect it against competitors, especially the sellers of NMN (nicotinamide mononucleotide), as the FDA's reversal of its previous decision regarding banning NMN as a dietary supplement opens the door for many companies to sell NMN at competitive prices. The management didn't remain idle; it cited a study published in the Cell Journal last January, including a comparison between Niagen and NMN. The result showed that taking Niagen led to more than double the levels of the NAD+ enzyme compared to NMN. To further strengthen its moat, NAGE announced a strategic acquisition last December of a patent portfolio from Queen's University Belfast covering the chemical formulations of NR salts, which gives the company the required flexibility to develop more stable and effective formulations for Niagen Plus injections, securing exclusive rights for the advanced forms of NAD+ boosters.

Financials & Outlook

During 2025, the company achieved net sales of $129.4 million with excellent growth of 30% compared to last year. This growth was primarily driven by its Tru Niagen brand, which grew by 27% to reach $97.7 million. The direct e-commerce sales also jumped to $74.1 million. On the other hand, the ingredients sector recorded tremendous growth of 45%, or $27.9 million. However, it's worth noting that pharmaceutical-grade Niagen ingredients achieved staggering growth of 123% to reach $3.8 million, which reflects a significant improvement in sales after the launch compared to 2024.

However, Q4 specifically showed a deceleration in growth rates to 16% compared to previous quarters, which exceeded the 30% barrier, but it explains the conservative approach from the management when presenting 2026 guidance.

Net income reached $17.4 million with a growth of 103%. Although this figure looks impressive, it includes non-operational elements, as the company recorded an accounting gain of $2 million due to a settlement of intellectual property fees. In addition, it recovered $1.3 million from a bad debt provision related to a legal settlement with Elysium Health company. However, this doesn't reduce the company's performance, as if we excluded these exceptional gains of $3.3 million, the core earnings would still be positive and confirm that the company has crossed the break-even point and started a sustainable profitability phase.

Management expects a revenue growth between 10% and 15% YoY. While this figure seems disappointing for an investor who is used to 30% growth, the CFO stated that the revenues will be focused in the second half, indicating that the management isn't basing its expectations on just the continued organic growth of Niagen capsules, but the year will also see the launch of their revolutionary product, Niagen Plus Home Injections. Therefore, I think the management chose to give easily achievable expectations through their core business while leaving the door open for huge surprises in the second half when the revenues of the telemedicine platform start to flow in.

Value Per Share

NAGE trades at around $4.5 with outstanding shares of 80.08 million, implying the company’s market cap is $359.5 million. Given the company has cash of $65 million, the EV is approximately $294.5 million. Considering the net income is $17.4 million in 2025, the stock trades at a trailing P/E of 22x and a forward P/E of 19x. While this multiple seems fair or a little high for a nutritional supplement company, it makes sense since the company will shift to a subscription model once it launches its telemedicine platform. Therefore, when comparing it with other HealthTech peers such as Hims & Hers (HIMS), which trades at a historical P/E exceeding 39x and a forward P/E & EV/Sales approximately the same as NAGE, we realize that the market started to give the company valuations like the telehealth companies. NAGE still has significant room for expansion and is protected by IP, as it holds exclusive commercial rights on its products, unlike HIMS, which has fierce competition in selling generics. I'm assuming Niagen will continue to improve operating leverage. By applying a premium forward P/E of 25x justified by its IP moat or taking into account $150 million in sales in 2026 with EV/Sales of 3x, which is the historical average, we will have an EV of about $450 million and an equity value of $515 million/$6-$8 per share, representing potential upside of 40% to 75%.

Risks To Consider

Niagen Bioscience heavily relies on W.R. Grace & Co.-Conn. as an exclusive and sole supplier of NRC material, as there is a contract that obliges W.R. Grace to be the exclusive manufacturer for NAGE until April 30, 2029. The problem is that W.R. Grace has a patent for the crystal structure of the material, which makes it almost impossible for NAGE to find an alternative in a short time. Therefore, if any dispute occurs between them or a disruption in the supply chains, this could lead to a complete paralysis in the company's sales.

According to section B503 of the FD&C Act, the NRC substance is included in the temporary list of bulk substances permitted for use in compounding formulations, as the company cooperates with FDA-registered facilities to distribute Niagen Plus. If the company fails to meet regulatory standards or the FDA changes its position on keeping the substance on the permissible list, this could close this segment, and thus our growth thesis for Niagen Plus could fail.

Final Thoughts

I believe that people's interest in maintaining their youth equals or even surpasses their interest in losing weight, as the ultimate goal is to be in good shape. The company seems to be in a good position thanks to the shift in consumer behavior regarding self-injections and its acquisition of QUB's patent portfolio to compound advanced NAD+ boosters. However, this depends on how effective its products are and how well people accept them. We should keep an eye on the launch schedule of Niagen Plus, which is supposed to be in H1 2026, and the potential market adoption of it. I believe any sign indicating a decrease in customers' acquisition cost or high retention rates will push the market to reprice the company as a health tech company and spark the stock to rally.

data.title: Very Bad News For Altria: This Dividend King Is Running Out Of Runway
data.description:
Back in 2023, I last covered Altria Group (MO), and at the time I rated it a hold, as its high yield and Dividend King status indicated that it had a lot going for it, but at the same time I had concerns about its underlying business model.

Since then, the stock has actually performed very well, delivering about 89% over that period of time, which outpaced the S&P 500. While I was on the sidelines due to my neutral outlook on the company and have therefore missed out on the strong total return performance, in today's article, I'm going to re-look at the investment thesis to determine whether or not it is worth buying today.

Still a Dividend King - But For How Long?

MO remains one of the most beloved dividend stocks in America, as its 56-year dividend growth streak puts it firmly in the dividend king category while also boasting a dividend yield of over 6%, making it a high-yielding stock. That being said, its strong recent performance means that its valuation is not as cheap as it once was. Is this strong recent stock price performance validated by improvements in the underlying business model?

The Marlboro Moat is Cracking

Well, MO does have a dominant position in the cigarette space, as its Marlboro brand holds about 40% of the U.S. cigarette retail market share, which is way above the competition. This business brings in the vast majority of its income, as its smokable product segment generates between 80–90% of its total operating income. However, this business has not really grown its revenue much at all in quite some time because it has had to increase its prices fairly aggressively in order to offset its steep volume declines.

Its customer base is overwhelmingly older generations that are gradually dying off, while younger demographics are not engaging in cigarette-smoking behavior at the same rate that previous generations did. In fact, domestic cigarette volumes have declined by 9.5% in 2025. The U.S. overall smoking rate has fallen from a whopping 42.4% in 1965 to just 11.6% by 2022, and the trend continues to point towards even lower smoking rates. On top of that, Marlboro's total retail share is also declining. It stood at a commanding 50% in 2018 and has declined to about 45% by the end of 2025, with its retail share dropping to below 40% in Q4 2025 for the first time ever.

Squeezed From Both Ends

Part of the reason for this is that the company has leaned heavily on price increases to sustain its profit growth, while inflationary pressures are squeezing the low-income consumers, which has pushed them to adopt deeply discounted cigarettes more aggressively. MO has been using a basic brand to try to help retain these customers, but it is still reducing its profit margins. Thus, MO is now being squeezed on both fronts, both from declining overall demand for the product itself as well as increasing pricing pressures.

A Costly History of Diversification Failures

The company is also seeing its oral tobacco segment stumble, as its retail share fell from 36.1% to 29.6% year over year in Q4 this past year. When you combine this weakness with the fact that MO has struggled with diversification efforts in the past, as its $12.8 billion JUUL investment was a massive loss for shareholder value, the $1.8 billion Cronos cannabis investment also appears to have destroyed shareholder value, and even its own nicotine pouches, despite their growth, remain a small segment that they are not able to come close to putting a dent into the decline in the cigarette business. Even the NJOY acquisition that they sunk $2.75 billion into ultimately led to a $1.3 billion non-cash impairment charge in Q4 2025.

This gives me great caution that MO will be able to diversify effectively away from its cigarette business. Meanwhile, though they have outperformed my expectations in sustaining profits due to price increases in their cigarette business, this too seems to be nearing the end of its runway due to pricing pressures and continued volume declines.

Balance Sheet & Growth Outlook: Red Flags Ahead

All this will likely put significant pressure on the company's ability to sustain its strong balance sheet and grow its dividend at a strong clip moving forward. The balance sheet still contains substantial debt from the JUUL purchase, with total debt to EBITDA at 2.0 times. The good news is that these debt maturities are well laddered through 2038, with no single year exceeding $2 billion in maturities. The company's $10.3 billion Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) stake is performing fairly well and delivers nice dividends to its cash flow statement.

Still, at a forward price-to-earnings ratio in the low teens, which is near the high end of its recent valuation range and its increasingly challenging growth outlook, it is hard to make the case for MO here. In fact, analyst consensus estimates only see revenue growing at a 0.6% CAGR through 2030, EBITDA growing at a 0.9% CAGR over that same period, and earnings per share growing at a 2.7% CAGR over that time.

Why the 6% Yield Isn't Enough to Buy This Shrinking Ice Cube

Thus, we are looking at what will likely be at best inflation-level growth, and quite possibly less than that, meaning that MO's shrinking ice cube in terms of volumes is likely at an inflection point of being about to show up in its bottom line as well, as net of inflation, MO is likely going to be a shrinking ice cube in the near future. While that does not break the investment thesis entirely, a dividend yield of a bit over 6% is not enough to entice me to adopt that sort of valuation outlook, especially given that its next 12-month price-to-earnings ratio of 11.7 times is well above its three-year average of 9.8 times.

This means that valuation multiple expansion is unlikely to be a material tailwind moving forward. Therefore, I simply do not see a clear path to double-digit annualized total returns moving forward, and that MO is likely to deliver mid-single-digit annualized total returns moving forward.

Overall, while MO does have a wide moat in its core business, that moat is showing some cracks, and the growth story appears to be running out of runway. Therefore, MO is likely going to be at best a bond proxy in the coming years, which puts it firmly into the hold category for me. While the company has been able to outrun my previous pessimism by posting strong returns in recent years, it is increasingly difficult for me to see a bullish scenario for it in the future. Therefore, I remain on the sidelines and am pursuing other high-yielding, high-quality dividend growth opportunities in my 7–8% yielding total return outperforming and below-market beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.
