data.title: Intel: Turnaround Is Real, But The Valuations Went Bonkers (Upgrade)
data.description:
Intel Proved Me So Wrong!

The case of Intel Corporation (INTC) is one that offers me a timely reminder why things could change quickly in the semiconductor space.

With INTC moving quickly to retake highs that we last saw in April 2021, it has even surpassed my wildest imagination when I decided to downgrade Intel stock in January.

I acknowledged Intel's turnaround, but I didn't give enough thought to the possibility that the market wants to take it even higher.

At this point, the valuations have also reached a stage where I find it conflating, but the market doesn't really bother about what I think, anyway.

INTC's price action has never looked better over the past few months. When compared to its semiconductor peers (SMH), I believe you could see the robust uptrending print that defines the optimism poured out into the stock.

It's getting clear that the market is now convinced that there is a fundamental shift in Intel's recovery. One that investors appear very keen to keep pounding the table on.

Intel: The Tide Has Turned To CPUs

The fact remains that Intel is still the market share leader in data center CPU, with >60% share. Sure, that gap with rivals has narrowed considerably over the years, putting pressure on its core business.

Yet, with the Agentic AI era now firmly establishing the importance of the CPU, Intel's opportunity to capitalize has surely arrived.

Arm's (ARM) decision to enter the CPU space is a timely one. As CEO Rene Haas opined, the "rise of AI Agents" is pivotal to the current phenomenon underscoring the return of the core business in CPUs.

While I don't think it has invalidated Nvidia's (NVDA) AI chips business, it has also refreshed the competitive moat for Intel's stronghold in the CPU slate, reminding us that they are far from being made obsolete.

And what’s so exciting is that these signals aren’t even theoretical to begin with.

They are already being felt, as recent signals show “CPU usage is accelerating again to support AI training and inference beyond head nodes.”

We are looking at an inflection point for CPU gains here.

And, one in which I believe will place Intel firmly in the catbird seat to capture these highly beneficial economics as it looks to regain the bullish narrative from undecided investors like me, right?

It’s the age of the CPU.

This makes sense why the market went bonkers on Intel stock. That ties in nicely with all the Claude Code fever going on right now.

And Anthropic (ANTHRO) is reportedly or rumored to be short of compute capacity.

OpenAI (OPENAI) thinks that Anthropic’s relative lack of ambition in stuffing itself with more compute has now left it strategically disadvantaged.

Could it now create the opening for OpenAI to regain control of the agentic conversation, and wrestle it away from Anthropic’s control?

Likewise for Intel.

But, Has The Market Gone Way Overboard?

INTC has undoubtedly outperformed Nvidia (NVDA) over the past year. But, when you consider the respective free cash flow margins, they aren’t even close.

Why is the market willing to give so much benefit of the doubt to Intel right here, right now? It's baffling to say the least.

At least for Anthropic, its annualized revenue run rate crossed $30B, as compared to the $25B that OpenAI reportedly garnered.

However, the fact is that the market is looking forward, right?

And that means that the runway for Intel to regain control of the narrative has arguably improved by leaps and bounds, and that’s just for the products side of the house.

And the forward EPS growth outlook for Intel is now in tune with Nvidia over the next two years (>50% average growth), although one could say that INTC’s forward earnings multiple of 125x has such a distinct disconnect when compared to NVDA’s 23.7x.

Like, seriously! What am I not getting right?

Both companies are expected to grow at roughly the same rates, but only one has managed >200% return in the past year.

Something must be missing in the jigsaw puzzle that I haven’t quite solved. What could it be?

Ah ha... I guess you could say Intel Foundry.

Intel Foundry Now Front And Center

I guess even Nvidia investors are confused. The promises baked into that very high INTC multiple contrast sharply with the entrenched dominance led by Nvidia’s systems, but accorded a much lower multiple.

Foundry is unquestionably the biggest optionality for Intel, yeah?

And this twin pillar of growth, CPUs and chip production, is now front and center.

If you want to find an American chipmaker that is integrated on the product front, chipmaking, and advanced packaging, Intel is really front and center.

The tie up with Elon Musk (TSLA) (SPACE) on Terafab is still in the very early stages.

I’m not even sure when we will even get a tape out, much less think about risk production. Does anyone have any idea?

If anything, a five-year period before ramping isn’t unreasonable, right?

And then, we have to think about whether Musk is merely relying on Intel’s capabilities in chipmaking before he vertically integrates that in-house.

I bet he’s thinking about doing most things in-house, especially for chipmaking. But that’s still a massive undertaking, and thus, Intel should still play a primary role for the foreseeable future.

And buying back Fab 34 from Apollo Global (APO) obviously lends credence to the foundry story, demonstrating the resolve and eagerness of Intel in getting back to business in foundry, while also “[reinjecting] operating leverage” back to its foundry business.

These steps create a credible long-term bull case for Intel’s chipmaking capabilities, and could send them back to the pantheon of chipmaking greats that they used to be, right?

If Elon Musk doesn’t think TSMC (TSM) can give him enough production capacity while the rest of the customers also compete for wafer allocations, then Intel has a really solid opening this time.

The only thing is, we still need to see firm commitments on 18A, even though the market’s enthusiasm suggests that they are probably waiting for some announcements already.

Can the advanced packaging discussions reportedly with Amazon (AMZN), and Google (GOOGL) translate into notable deals and be good enough to convince the market that anything short of 18A commitments or orders is good enough?

If they do, these might offer Intel more than just hope when thinking about having possible customers for 18A, even though I don’t think management sees it as the base case.

For now, the strategy is still to try and fill external commitments for 14A, but the market might have already gone above and beyond looking for possibilities for 18A orders.

Nevertheless, 18A-P will still represent a very encouraging start, and I think the market is definitely placing hopeful bets on it actualizing.

Intel management is clearly trying to calibrate its penetration into the foundry space amid extremely tight advanced packaging capacity for the agentic era.

And Intel believes that even if someone else’s wafers are used, the company can still do the advanced packaging business, and one that is supposedly more efficient.

So, that opening is real, and something that the market is really trying to price in. Although, I think Wall Street still needs more conviction before it will adjust the earnings growth to reflect already well-elevated multiples.

If these announcements don’t come soon enough, I fear that such high multiples don’t quite have the execution to justify them, right?

Buy Or Sell INTC Stock?

Nonetheless, INTC’s chart is one that really fascinates me right now.

Take a closer look.

April 2021 was when INTC peaked and then entered a multi-year downward spiral that finally culminated in a bottom at around the $20 level.

That was April 2025. A four year breakdown, which I guess probably eviscerated many of the bulls who lost hope with INTC’s thesis.

One year on from those “hopeless” days, Intel has rediscovered its mojo, outperforming semiconductor peers since then. Could it still go higher?

Incidentally, Intel stock has also inched back toward the $70 level.

That level previously defined where sellers came back and walloped the stock in 2021, disallowing it from going anywhere higher in the next four years.

Five years on, Intel stock appears to have been reinvigorated, and the bad memories appear to have been (nearly) fully extinguished from the print.

No longer chasing after AI GPUs that aren’t really its forte, as the market is now moving back toward CPUs in the Agentic AI surge, which is Intel’s core business.

Struggling to keep up with TSMC’s tech no longer seems to be such a structural disadvantage any longer, as the world mulls not even having enough production capacity as the bigger downside, especially in advanced packaging.

Intel’s fortunes have taken such a dramatic turnaround that it has also surprised me thoroughly, as it flipped my narrative on its head.

The trend bias favoring INTC over the semi peers indicates that this is a multi-month recovery that may pan out to be a multi-year one.

However, with valuations now having moved well ahead of execution, it will now be up to CEO Lip-Bu Tan and his team to show me whether the story is justified.

So for me to go fully bullish right here, right now, does seem a tad too late for me, as the market came back with such a vengeance to invalidate my previous rating.

That said, a bearish rating is now looking stale and no longer tenable to validate.

Persisting with it would have needed me to go against the dominant trend, which is against my nature of how price action (and the market) works.

Rating: Upgrade to Hold.

data.title: M&T Bank: Robust Performance In Q1
data.description:
In today’s column, we continue our coverage of the regional bank earnings season by checking in on M&T Bank (MTB). As one of the larger and more sophisticated regional players in our coverage universe, M&T Bank often provides the first real look at how the commercial and industrial sectors are weathering the current interest rate environment and recent economic concerns stemming from the Iran War. Of course, the stock has seen some interesting price action leading up to this report, having faced some pressure alongside the broader financial sector over the last month. It has recovered here some in April with the market rebound. After digging through the just-reported Q1 results, we see the bank as continuing to be resilient with disciplined capital management. Despite the broader macroeconomic concerns, M&T Bank managed to deliver a report that exceeded expectations on several fronts. We think the bank stock is a buy. In this column, we will break down the key metrics and discuss what this means for investors. Let us discuss.

Headline results

M&T Bank reported headline results that significantly outpaced what analysts were looking for. The bank reported EPS of $4.13, up nicely from the $3.38 reported in the same quarter last year. On an adjusted basis, the beat was even more pronounced, coming in at $4.18, well above the consensus estimate of $4.01. This was driven by a nice top line move. Total revenue was $2.44 billion, up 6% year-over-year. This revenue growth was primarily fueled by a 3% rise in net interest income and a very strong 13% increase in non-interest income. Solid results overall here. It is impressive to see this kind of top-line growth at a time when many regional peers are struggling to maintain momentum, though it is early in our coverage of Q1 earnings season.

Margins expanded

One of the most critical indicators we follow for the banking sector is the net interest margin, and M&T Bank did not disappoint here. A good number of regional banks are seeing a flattening yield curve that compresses their margins, though funding costs have improved some with the cost of deposits coming down. M&T Bank actually saw its net interest margin expand. The margin came in at 3.71% in Q1, a sequential 2 basis point improvement from the 3.69% in Q4 and a 5 basis point move up from the 3.66% seen a year ago. The bank's asset yields are currently rising faster than its funding costs, which is exactly what you want to see in this part of the cycle. Management’s ability to price loans effectively while managing deposit costs going forward is something we will monitor. It is also worth noting that the bank’s outlook for 2026 net interest income remains solid, with guidance calling for $7.2 billion to $7.35 billion for 2026.

Loans and deposits

Loans and deposits are the engines of any regional bank, and the trends here offer a unique look at the economic health of the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic corridors where M&T Bank operates. On the lending side, average total loans grew to $138.4 billion, which was a 3% increase from last year and a 1% sequential increase. The growth was led by commercial and industrial lending, as well as residential real estate and consumer loans. This suggests that business owners and consumers in the bank's core markets remain active and confident enough to continue seeking credit. Interestingly, we did see a decline in commercial real estate balances. This is something we have seen across the industry as banks intentionally manage their exposure to certain sectors that may be facing higher scrutiny.

Average deposits ended the quarter at $164.3 billion, a slight dip from the $165.1 billion reported in the sequential quarter, but they remain higher than the prior year levels. We are seeing a general stabilization in deposits across the regional space, and while there is always some competition for rate-sensitive funds, M&T Bank’s deposits have held mostly firm of late

Asset quality

Now, we must look at asset quality, which is where we often find the first signs of trouble if the economy is starting to soften. M&T Bank’s credit metrics remain very resilient. Net charge-offs for the quarter were 0.31% of average loans, which is actually a slight improvement from the previous year. Non-accrual loans also declined to 0.89% of total loans. These are very healthy numbers. However, we did note that the provision for credit losses was $140 million, a bit higher than the $125 million seen in the prior quarter. This increase in the provision is likely more of a cautionary move or a reflection of loan growth rather than a sign of actual credit stress. The allowance for credit losses was maintained at 1.53% of total loans. In our view, the bank is well-prepared for any potential downturn, and the current credit profile does not raise any red flags.

Shareholder-friendly

We also want to point out that as a bank, it has dividends each quarter, which is a nice bonus, and the company is buying back stock. During the first quarter alone, the bank repurchased $1.25 billion of its common stock. This is a massive amount of capital being returned to investors, and it shows the high level of confidence management has in the bank’s future.

Looking ahead

Looking ahead, the bank is performing at a very high level. Management has issued solid guidance for the remainder of 2026, and while we will continue to monitor the broader economy for signs of weakness, M&T Bank is positioned well. The combination of margin expansion, disciplined loan growth, and significant shareholder returns justifies a buy.
